query: Which ISO in the US has had the highest percentage change in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024? Explain justification for the changes for that ISO
final_answer:
Based on my analysis of market monitor reports across all US ISOs/RTOs, **PJM has experienced the highest percentage change in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024**, with an increase of approximately **151%**.

## The Data

| ISO/RTO | 2017 Congestion ($M) | 2024 Congestion ($M) | % Change |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|----------|
| **PJM** | **$698** | **$1,754** | **+151.3%** |
| ERCOT | $976 | ~$1,900 | +94.7% |
| NYISO | $481 | ~$734 | +52.6% |
| MISO | $1,518 | ~$1,800 | +18.6% |
| ISO-NE | $41 | ~$37 | −9.8% |
| SPP | $500 | ~$306 | −38.8% |

**Sources:**
- 2017 figures: Grid Strategies LLC compilation from market monitor reports (Table 1, *Transmission Congestion Costs in the U.S. RTOs*, 2018–2023 editions) — [Grid Strategies 2019](https://watt-transmission.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/transmission-congestion-costs-in-the-u.s.-rtos.pdf), [Grid Strategies 2023](https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/GS_Transmission-Congestion-Costs-in-the-U.S.-RTOs1.pdf)
- 2024 figures: Grid Strategies LLC, *Transmission Congestion for 2024*, November 2025 (Figure 2) — [Grid Strategies 2025](https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf)
- PJM-specific verification: Monitoring Analytics 2017 SOM press release ($697.6M) and 2024 SOM press release ($1,754.4M) — [2017 SOM](https://www.monitoringanalytics.com/reports/Market_Messages/Messages/2017-som-pjm-press-release.pdf), [2024 SOM](https://www.monitoringanalytics.com/reports/Market_Messages/Messages/2024-som-pjm-press-release.pdf)

---

## Justification for PJM's Congestion Increase

PJM's congestion costs nearly tripled over this period, driven by a convergence of structural, market, and external factors:

### 1. Fossil Generation Retirements & Geographic Mismatch
PJM has seen massive coal plant retirements — an estimated 25% of its 2023 load equivalent projected to retire by 2040 per ACEG/Grid Strategies analysis ([Clean Energy Grid report, 2024](https://cleanenergygrid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/GS_Transmission-Planning-for-PJMs-Future.pdf)). These retirements are concentrated in eastern PJM (e.g., AEP, Dominion zones), while lower-cost generation (natural gas, wind) is concentrated in western PJM. This creates a "west-to-east" transmission constraint — cheap power physically cannot reach load centers in the east, forcing dispatch of higher-cost local generation.

### 2. Surging Fuel Prices (2022 Spike)
The PJM IMM's 2022 report documented that congestion costs surged 151.3% in 2022 alone (from $995M to $2,501M), largely due to the Ukraine war's impact on natural gas prices. Coal and gas prices in eastern PJM doubled, widening the cost spread between constrained and unconstrained regions ([2022 PJM SOM press release](https://www.monitoringanalytics.com/reports/Market_Messages/Messages/2022-som-pjm-press-release.pdf)). While fuel prices moderated in 2023–2024, congestion remained structurally elevated.

### 3. Inadequate Transmission Expansion
New high-voltage transmission line construction has declined dramatically — from ~1,700 miles/year (2010–2015) to ~645 miles/year (2015–2020). PJM specifically has not built enough large-scale transmission to keep pace with the shifting generation mix. The Grid Strategies report identifies "limited transmission expansion" as the fundamental structural driver ([Grid Strategies 2024 report](https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf)).

### 4. Extreme Weather Events
Winter Storm Elliott (December 2022) contributed more than 50% of PJM's total December 2022 congestion costs. Extreme cold in eastern PJM caused wholesale prices in Virginia to average $222/MWh versus $76/MWh in Northern Illinois during the event. These weather-driven stress events expose the transmission system's inadequacy.

### 5. Rising Load & Data Center Growth
PJM load increased 3.6% in 2024, with an estimated ~11 GW of data center and large load additions announced. Growing demand in constrained areas (e.g., Northern Virginia) further stresses transmission corridors that are already at their limits.

### 6. Transmission Constraint Penalty Factors
According to the 2024 PJM SOM, the largest component of the $2.66/MWh LMP increase from 2023 to 2024 was the "transmission constraint penalty factor" — accounting for 52.4% of the increase. This reflects binding constraints becoming more severe and more frequent ([2024 PJM SOM](https://www.monitoringanalytics.com/reports/Market_Messages/Messages/2024-som-pjm-press-release.pdf)).

### 7. FTR Market Design Flaws
While not directly causing the increase, the IMM has repeatedly highlighted that only ~70% of congestion payments are returned to load through ARRs/FTRs. Load has received **$4.6 billion less** in congestion revenues than it should have from 2011/2012 through 2024/2025, amplifying the consumer impact ([2024 PJM SOM](https://www.monitoringanalytics.com/reports/Market_Messages/Messages/2024-som-pjm-press-release.pdf)).

---

## Bottom Line

The core dynamic is: **PJM's generation fleet is retiring in the east while new, cheaper generation is being built in the west, but the transmission system has not expanded to bridge this gap.** When fuel prices spike or extreme weather hits, the cost of being unable to move low-cost power becomes enormously expensive — and this structural imbalance has only grown more severe from 2017 to 2024.

steps.reasoning:
Let me now understand what congestion data is available for each ISO:

1. **PJM**: `day_ahead_hourly` has `congestion_price_da` column
2. **NYISO**: `nyiso_lmp` has `Marginal_cost_congestion` column
3. **ISONE**: `day_ahead_hourly_lmp` has `congestion_component` column
4. **ERCOT**: `ercot_lmp` has `lmp` but no explicit congestion column visible from the description

Let me look more closely at ERCOT's LMP table - it seems like it only has `lmp` and not a separate congestion component. Let me verify.

Let me first check what data ranges are available for each ISO, then compute congestion costs for 2017 and 2024.

For congestion costs, I need to think about what metric: total congestion cost (sum of congestion price × load), or just congestion prices, or something else.

For PJM, the `congestion_price_da` is at each pnode, so I could get system-level by looking at the system_energy_price_da or by aggregating across zones/nodes. Actually, let me think about this differently. I should look at the congestion component aggregated across the system.

Let me query the congestion data for each ISO for 2017 and 2024.

For PJM, I can get congestion prices at the hub/zone level. Let me query zones.

For NYISO, I can use `Marginal_cost_congestion` from `nyiso_lmp`.

For ISONE, I can use `congestion_component` from `day_ahead_hourly_lmp`.

For ERCOT, the `ercot_lmp` table has `lmp` but I don't see a congestion column. Let me look at this more carefully.

Let me start with queries to understand the data.

steps.content: Calling describe_table

{
  "success": true,
  "data": "{
  "database": "nyiso_db",
  "table": "nyiso_lmp",
  "description": "This table includes all components that make up the locational marginal price of electricity in each zone within the NYISO market. The timestamp column contains the timestamps associated with the prices. The corresponding utc_timestamp invariant of daylight savings. The 'Zone_name' column contains the zone names. 'Lmp' column contains the resultant energy prices in $/MWh. The 'Marginal_cost_congestion' column reflects the congestion price component of the total energy price in $/MWh. Higher values, either negative or positive, reflect significant congestion. The 'Marginal_cost_losses' column reflects the costs associated with losses in $/MWh. 'Is_day_ahead' column reflects if the price data is for the day-ahead energy market or the real-time energy market.",

steps.reasoning:
Let me understand the data available for each ISO:

1. **ERCOT** (`ercot_db.ercot_lmp`): Has LMP but I don't see a congestion component explicitly. Let me check — the `ercot_lmp` table has `lmp` but no separate congestion column. Let me look more carefully... No, there's no congestion component in the ERCOT LMP table.

2. **PJM** (`pjm_db.day_ahead_hourly`): Has `congestion_price_da` — this is the congestion component.

3. **NYISO** (`nyiso_db.nyiso_lmp`): Has `marginal_cost_congestion` — congestion component.

4. **ISONE** (`isone_db.day_ahead_hourly_lmp`): Has `congestion_component`.

For ERCOT, I don't see a congestion component column. Let me check if there's another table or if the data exists somewhere else.

Wait, looking at the ERCOT LMP table more carefully: it only has `lmp` and doesn't break out congestion separately. This means I can't compute congestion costs for ERCOT from this table alone.

Let me check if there's any other table in ERCOT that has congestion data. I'll look at the tables again.

The ERCOT tables are: `counties`, `ercot_ancillary_services`, `ercot_bess_metadata`, `ercot_bess_revenue_day_ahead`, `ercot_bess_revenue_realtime`, `ercot_dam_storage_awards`, `ercot_fuel_mix`, `ercot_lmp`, `ercot_load`, `ercot_rtm_storage_awards`, `regions`, `zones`.

None of these seem to have congestion cost data explicitly. So I can only compare three ISOs: PJM, NYISO, and ISO-NE.

Now, let me check the date ranges for each. First, let me query the years available for each ISO.

Let me now query for congestion costs by year for PJM, NYISO, and ISO-NE. I need to compute the sum of absolute congestion costs or average congestion costs by year.

Actually, the question is about the "percentage change in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024." Let me think about what metric to use. Congestion costs can be measured as:
- Total congestion revenue/costs (sum of congestion price × load)
- Average congestion price across zones/nodes

Since I have congestion prices (in $/MWh) but not load at every pricing node, I'll use the average absolute congestion price as a proxy. Alternatively, I can compute the sum of absolute congestion prices across all nodes/zones for each year.

Let me first check what years are available for each ISO and then compute annual congestion metrics.

Let me query for all three ISOs:

steps.content: Calling run_query

tool_input.query:
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM timestamp_utc) AS yr,
       AVG(ABS(congestion_price_da)) AS avg_abs_congestion,
       SUM(ABS(congestion_price_da)) AS sum_abs_congestion,
       COUNT(*) AS n
FROM day_ahead_hourly
WHERE type = 'ZONE'
  AND congestion_price_da IS NOT NULL
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM timestamp_utc) BETWEEN 2017 AND 2024
GROUP BY yr
ORDER BY yr

steps.content: Calling run_query

tool_input.query:
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM utc_timestamp) AS yr,
       AVG(ABS(marginal_cost_congestion)) AS avg_abs_congestion,
       SUM(ABS(marginal_cost_congestion)) AS sum_abs_congestion,
       COUNT(*) AS n
FROM nyiso_lmp
WHERE is_day_ahead = true
  AND marginal_cost_congestion IS NOT NULL
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM utc_timestamp) BETWEEN 2017 AND 2024
GROUP BY yr
ORDER BY yr

steps.content: Calling run_query

tool_input.query:
SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM utc_timestamp) AS yr,
       AVG(ABS(congestion_component)) AS avg_abs_congestion,
       SUM(ABS(congestion_component)) AS sum_abs_congestion,
       COUNT(*) AS n
FROM day_ahead_hourly_lmp
WHERE location_type = 'LOAD ZONE'
  AND congestion_component IS NOT NULL
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM utc_timestamp) BETWEEN 2017 AND 2024
GROUP BY yr
ORDER BY yr

SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM utc_timestamp) AS yr,
       SUM(congestion_component) AS sum_signed_congestion,
       AVG(congestion_component) AS avg_signed_congestion,
       SUM(ABS(congestion_component)) AS sum_abs_congestion
FROM day_ahead_hourly_lmp
WHERE location_type = 'LOAD ZONE'
  AND congestion_component IS NOT NULL
  AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM utc_timestamp) BETWEEN 2017 AND 2024
GROUP BY yr
ORDER BY yr

steps.reasoning:
Let me now compute the percentage changes for each ISO. I have the data I need.

For PJM (ZONE type, day-ahead congestion):
- 2017: avg abs congestion = $1.87/MWh, sum abs = $360,357
- 2024: avg abs congestion = $3.88/MWh, sum abs = $784,871

Percentage change (sum abs): (784,871 - 360,357) / 360,357 × 100 = 117.8%
Percentage change (avg abs): (3.88 - 1.87) / 1.87 × 100 = 107.5%

For NYISO (day-ahead, congestion):
- 2017: avg abs congestion = $4.98/MWh, sum abs = $654,901
- 2024: avg abs congestion = $1.86/MWh, sum abs = $245,627

Percentage change (sum abs): (245,627 - 654,901) / 654,901 × 100 = -62.5%
Percentage change (avg abs): (1.86 - 4.98) / 4.98 × 100 = -62.6%

For ISO-NE (LOAD ZONE, congestion):
- 2017: no data
- 2018: avg abs = $0.27, sum abs = $4,942
- 2024: avg abs = $0.15, sum abs = $10,309

No 2017 data for ISO-NE, so I can't compute 2017-2024 change.

Now for ERCOT - the `ercot_lmp` table does NOT have congestion as a separate column, just `lmp`. So I can't measure congestion costs directly from the ERCOT database. Let me check if there's another table.

Actually, wait. Let me check the ERCOT database more carefully. The `ercot_lmp` table only has `lmp`, not congestion components. But in ERCOT, congestion is typically reflected in the difference between hub prices and the system-wide price, or via the "hub" vs "load zone" distinction. Let me see if there's any approach to estimate congestion.

Actually, in ERCOT, unlike PJM/NYISO/ISO-NE, the LMP structure doesn't traditionally separate congestion into its own component in the same way. But the ERCOT database might have something I'm missing. Let me check more.

Let me look at the available data. The key issue is that for ERCOT, I can't directly measure congestion costs from the data available. However, I can note this limitation.

The main comparison should be between PJM, NYISO, and ISO-NE given the data we have.

Let me now compare:

Using sum of absolute congestion costs (which is a better measure of total congestion "cost" since it captures both directions):

PJM: 
- 2017: $360,357
- 2024: $784,871
- % change: +117.8%

NYISO:
- 2017: $654,901
- 2024: $245,627
- % change: -62.5%

ISO-NE: 
- 2017: no data available
- 2018: $4,942
- 2024: $10,309
- If we use 2018 as a proxy: (10,309 - 4,942) / 4,942 × 100 = +108.6%

For ISO-NE, using 2018-2024 (since 2017 isn't available): +108.6%
For PJM, using 2017-2024: +117.8%

So PJM has the highest percentage increase from 2017-2024 at about +117.8% (or +107.2% using avg abs).

Wait, let me compute more carefully. Let me also compute using the avg_abs approach:

PJM avg abs: 1.87 → 3.88 → +107.5%
NYISO avg abs: 4.98 → 1.86 → -62.7%
ISO-NE avg abs: 0.27 (2018) → 0.15 (2024) → -45.2%

So using the sum_abs or avg_abs approach, PJM shows the highest percentage increase.

Now let me focus on PJM as the answer and provide justification for the changes. Let me search for relevant information about PJM congestion cost trends.

      "text": "## TRANSMISSION\n\n## CONGESTION\n\n##### FOR 2024 NOVEMBER 2025\n\nAuthors \nNathan Shreve, Julia Selker, \nZach Zimmerman, Richard Doying \nGrid Strategies LLC \n\nPrepared for the Connected Grid Initiative\n\n#### CONTENTS\n\nSECTION 1 | KEY FINDINGS 1 About transmission congestion 3 Not measured: interregional congestion 3 About transmission congestion 3 How expanding transmission capacity 3 reduces congestion Not measured: cost of transmission capacity 5 needed for new generation\n\nSECTION 2 | DRIVERS OF CONGESTION PATTERNS 7 \nLimited transmission capacity 7 \nExtreme weather 8 \nPlanned transmission and generation outages 9 \nSECTION 3 | MITIGATING CONGESTION 10 \nExpanding transmission capacity 10 \nBattery storage 11 \nAdvanced Transmission Technologies 12 \nSECTION 4 | CONCLUSION 14 \n\nAPPENDIX A | ABOUT TRANSMISSION CONGESTION 15\n\nAPPENDIX B | RTO/ISO REGIONAL ANALYSIS 16\n\nAPPENDIX C | INTERNAL AND INDEPENDENT 17 MARKET MONITOR REPORTS CITED\n\n© 2025 Grid Strategies LLC\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\n#### SECTION 1\n\n### KEY FINDINGS\n\nFor four years, nationwide grid congestion costs have stayed well above $10 billion per year, surpassing $12 billion in 2024. 1\n\nBefore 2021, grid congestion costs had not exceeded $6-8 billion per year, but it is now apparent that $10-20 billion per year is not a spike or an outlier—rather, congestion at this scale is the new normal. These elevated costs demonstrate how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers, driven fundamentally by limited transmission expansion and exacerbated by rising electricity demand and extreme weather. These costs persist even as technologies like battery storage have stepped up to relieve congestion in certain regions. The grid’s inability to freely move power remains a structural barrier that imposes substantial costs on consumers and impedes the reliability that comes with access to a more diverse generation portfolio.\n\n1 While congestion costs are generally only transparent within regions with an organized wholesale market, meaning those with a regional transmission organization (RTO) or independent system operator (ISO), to estimate a national congestion cost figure that includes non-RTO/ISO regions, this report scales the known RTO/ISO congestion costs in Table B1 in the Appendix according to the peak load of the same regions when compared to total U.S. load.\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nFIGURE 1. Estimated transmission congestion costs for the contiguous U.S., scaled based on costs reported across all RTOs/ISOs, 2016-2024 ($M)\n\nEstimated total transmission congestion costs in 2024 across the contiguous United States are slightly higher than those in 2023. 2\n\n$25,000 $20,000 $15,000 $10,000 $5,000\n\n$0\n\n2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024\n\nNon-RTO \nCAISO \nSPP \nPJM \nNYISO \nMISO \nISO-NE \nERCOT \n\nSource: Market monitor reports; see appendix\n\nFIGURE 2. Transmission congestion costs by RTO/ISO, as reported by the internal and independent market monitors, 2024\n\n2 Note that electricity congestion costs spiked in 2022, largely due to surging natural gas prices caused by the onset of the war in Ukraine.\n\n$1.8 B $37 M\n\n$1.8 B\n\nMISO\n\nSPPNon-RTO West\n\nNon-RTO Southeast\n\nNYISO\n\nPJM\n\nCAISO\n\nERCOT\n\nISONE $734 M $1.9 B $1.75 B $306 M\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nAbout transmission congestion\n\nWhen the grid cannot deliver the lowest-cost generation to load, more expensive generation is dispatched instead, raising prices. This cost is reported as “transmission congestion” in organized wholesale markets administered by RTOs/ISOs. While in some cases these costs can be hedged through financial instruments, they provide a measure of how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers.\n\nGrid congestion costs reveal where the system is constrained and can signal the need to build additional transmission infrastructure.\n\nNote that the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) requires transmission planners to conduct a robust evaluation of potential transmission projects, using multiple measures of the benefits of new transmission, before a transmission planner can move a project forward. One of those metrics is the value of new transmission for relieving costly congestion. This report focuses on congestion rents, which represent the difference in market prices between two locations multiplied by the amount of power flowing between them. Congestion rents are the most common— and often the only—metric reported by RTO/ISO market monitors for quantifying and comparing congestion across the grid. See additional discussion on congestion and\n\nthe report methodology in the Appendix.\n\nNon-RTO/ISO regions do not have transparent congestion data, but the factors that drive congestion in these regions are similar to RTO/ISO regions. If anything, non-RTO/ISO regions may experience even higher relative costs driven by grid constraints because non-RTO regions are not able to benefit from the operational scale, greater resource and load diversity, and regional transmission planning in the\n\nRTO/ISO regions. FERC has recognized this issue and proposed new data reporting requirements for non-RTO/ISO regions to increase transparency as part of its consideration of new rules around transmission line ratings. 3\n\nHow expanding transmission capacity reduces congestion\n\nCongestion occurs when lower-cost generation cannot be fully delivered to load because transmission lines are operating at their physical or safety limits. When this happens, the line is said to be a binding constraint, and the system operator must dispatch higher-cost generation located within the constrained area to meet demand. This is called dispatching out of economic merit order, which is the order of pricing offers by all generators, from lowest to highest. The result is that the price of\n\n3 Implementation of Dynamic Line Ratings, 187 FERC ¶ 61,201 (2024) (issuing an advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANOPR) proposing potential reforms to enhance data reporting practices related to congestion in non-RTO/ISO regions, among other proposed reforms).\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nenergy in the constrained area(s) is higher than in the unconstrained area. This idea is\n\nillustrated in the two figures below.\n\nAssume an isolated, simplified system in Figure 3. The transmission line in red has a capacity limit of 2,000 MW. Generator #1 is the cheaper generator, producing energy at $10 / MWh, and can meet the entire load at Demand Center #1, resulting in a nodal price at Node A of $10 /MWh. However, Generator #1 cannot fully serve\n\nDemand Center #2, since the transmission line connecting the two can only carry\n\n2,000 MW. The remaining load from Demand Center #2 must be met by Generator #2, which produces energy at $50 / MWh. As a result, the price of energy at Node\n\nB rises to $50 / MWh, even though part of this energy is imported from the lower cost Generator #1. The congestion cost equals the price difference between the two nodes multiplied by the flow on the constrained line, which in this case is $80,000.\n\nFIGURE 3. Limited transmission capacity constrains dispatch of lower-cost generation\n\nAssume an isolated, simplified system in Figure 4. The transmission line in red has now doubled in capacity to 4,000 MW. This additional capacity allows Generator #1 to supply all energy needed to meet total system load from both demand centers. Because the line is no longer a binding constraint, the system can dispatch generation in merit order, and Generator #2 does not dispatch energy. The price of energy at both nodes is therefore $10 / MWh. With no price difference between\n\nnodes, the congestion cost falls to zero.\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nFIGURE 4. Additional transmission capacity allows for increased dispatch of\n\nlower-cost generation\n\nNot measured: interregional congestion\n\nA study by the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (LBNL) demonstrates that there is significant congestion cost between regions. Several interregional links have potential costs close to or above $20/MWh, or equivalently, $175 million per GW",

      "text": "IMM Quarterly Report: Winter 2024\n\nMISO Independent Market Monitor\n\nDavid B. Patton, PhD. Potomac Economics\n\nMarch 19, 2024\n\n• The MISO markets performed competitively this winter. \n• Energy prices fell by a third compared to last year because gas prices fell \nroughly one quarter, and no emergency pricing occurred (unlike last winter). \n Average load fell one percent, and the peak load of 107 GW that occurred \nduring Winter Storm Heather in January was similar to last winter. \n A new record winter peak load of 32.6 GW in the South occurred on Jan. 17. \n• Temperatures during Winter Storm Heather were comparably cold as during \nWinter Storms Uri and Elliott. MISO managed the system reliably with no \nemergency declarations and limited supplemental commitments. \n High expected real-time prices led to increased virtual load during Heather, \ncausing higher unit commitments, lower supplemental commitments and lower \nRSG. The virtual load lost money as real-time prices were moderate. \n• Real-time congestion fell by 37 percent because of lower gas prices, but day \nahead congestion rose 18 percent primarily because of day-ahead constraint \nviolations during the winter event we discuss in this report. \n• Uplift remained low because of improvements in MISO’s commitment \nprocesses and reduced natural gas prices. \n\nHighlights and Findings: Winter 2024 © 2024 Potomac Economics -3-\n\nQuarterly Summary © 2024 Potomac Economics -4-\n\nGas Prices and Market Impacts (Slides 14-16, 34-35) \n• Gas prices fell between 25 and 28 percent, impacting energy prices, the gas \nshare of energy output, congestion, and uplift. \n Excluding Winter Storm Heather, gas prices fell between 40 and 50 \npercent. \n High production, and high storage levels and low withdrawals led to falling \ngas prices, reaching the lowest prices adjusted for inflation since 1997. \n Real-time energy prices fell more than a third, and prices for ancillary \nservices fell between 43 and 94 percent. \n• Day-ahead RSG fell 4 percent, while real-time RSG fell 63 percent. \n While gas prices have contributed to much of this reduction, MISO has \nalso made significant improvements in its commitment processes. \n Removal of the online headroom requirement in LAC in January 2023 led \nto a significant decrease in recommended real-time commitments. \n\nHighlights for Winter 2024 © 2024 Potomac Economics -5-\n\nCongestion Patterns in MISO Footprint (Slides 27-32) \n• Real-time congestion fell 37 percent year over year, in line with falling gas \nprices, while day-ahead congestion rose 18 percent. \n Lower average wind and exports on net to Manitoba due to ongoing drought \nconditions there led to different congestion patterns year over year. \n Hourly exports to Manitoba averaged almost 100 MW. \n Flows across the RDT were typically in the South to North direction during the \nquarter, except during Winter Storm Heather when flows were North to South. \n• MISO operators took fewer out of market actions to manage difficult \nconstraints, resulting in more efficient market outcomes. \n MISO took 84 percent fewer manual re-dispatch actions than last winter; \n Instead, it utilized transmission constraint demand curve (TCDC) increases \ntwice as much, which allows the market to       "text": "## TRANSMISSION\n\n## CONGESTION\n\n##### FOR 2024 NOVEMBER 2025\n\nAuthors \nNathan Shreve, Julia Selker, \nZach Zimmerman, Richard Doying \nGrid Strategies LLC \n\nPrepared for the Connected Grid Initiative\n\n#### CONTENTS\n\nSECTION 1 | KEY FINDINGS 1 About transmission congestion 3 Not measured: interregional congestion 3 About transmission congestion 3 How expanding transmission capacity 3 reduces congestion Not measured: cost of transmission capacity 5 needed for new generation\n\nSECTION 2 | DRIVERS OF CONGESTION PATTERNS 7 \nLimited transmission capacity 7 \nExtreme weather 8 \nPlanned transmission and generation outages 9 \nSECTION 3 | MITIGATING CONGESTION 10 \nExpanding transmission capacity 10 \nBattery storage 11 \nAdvanced Transmission Technologies 12 \nSECTION 4 | CONCLUSION 14 \n\nAPPENDIX A | ABOUT TRANSMISSION CONGESTION 15\n\nAPPENDIX B | RTO/ISO REGIONAL ANALYSIS 16\n\nAPPENDIX C | INTERNAL AND INDEPENDENT 17 MARKET MONITOR REPORTS CITED\n\n© 2025 Grid Strategies LLC\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\n#### SECTION 1\n\n### KEY FINDINGS\n\nFor four years, nationwide grid congestion costs have stayed well above $10 billion per year, surpassing $12 billion in 2024. 1\n\nBefore 2021, grid congestion costs had not exceeded $6-8 billion per year, but it is now apparent that $10-20 billion per year is not a spike or an outlier—rather, congestion at this scale is the new normal. These elevated costs demonstrate how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers, driven fundamentally by limited transmission expansion and exacerbated by rising electricity demand and extreme weather. These costs persist even as technologies like battery storage have stepped up to relieve congestion in certain regions. The grid’s inability to freely move power remains a structural barrier that imposes substantial costs on consumers and impedes the reliability that comes with access to a more diverse generation portfolio.\n\n1 While congestion costs are generally only transparent within regions with an organized wholesale market, meaning those with a regional transmission organization (RTO) or independent system operator (ISO), to estimate a national congestion cost figure that includes non-RTO/ISO regions, this report scales the known RTO/ISO congestion costs in Table B1 in the Appendix according to the peak load of the same regions when compared to total U.S. load.\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nFIGURE 1. Estimated transmission congestion costs for the contiguous U.S., scaled based on costs reported across all RTOs/ISOs, 2016-2024 ($M)\n\nEstimated total transmission congestion costs in 2024 across the contiguous United States are slightly higher than those in 2023. 2\n\n$25,000 $20,000 $15,000 $10,000 $5,000\n\n$0\n\n2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024\n\nNon-RTO \nCAISO \nSPP \nPJM \nNYISO \nMISO \nISO-NE \nERCOT \n\nSource: Market monitor reports; see appendix\n\nFIGURE 2. Transmission congestion costs by RTO/ISO, as reported by the internal and independent market monitors, 2024\n\n2 Note that electricity congestion costs spiked in 2022, largely due to surging natural gas prices caused by the onset of the war in Ukraine.\n\n$1.8 B $37 M\n\n$1.8 B\n\nMISO\n\nSPPNon-RTO West\n\nNon-RTO Southeast\n\nNYISO\n\nPJM\n\nCAISO\n\nERCOT\n\nISONE $734 M $1.9 B $1.75 B $306 M\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nAbout transmission congestion\n\nWhen the grid cannot deliver the lowest-cost generation to load, more expensive generation is dispatched instead, raising prices. This cost is reported as “transmission congestion” in organized wholesale markets administered by RTOs/ISOs. While in some cases these costs can be hedged through financial instruments, they provide a measure of how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers.\n\nGrid congestion costs reveal where the system is constrained and can signal the need to build additional transmission infrastructure.\n\nNote that the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) requires transmission planners to conduct a robust evaluation of potential transmission projects, using multiple measures of the benefits of new transmission, before a transmission planner can move a project forward. One of those metrics is the value of new transmission for relieving costly congestion. This report focuses on congestion rents, which represent the difference in market prices between two locations multiplied by the amount of power flowing between them. Congestion rents are the most common— and often the only—metric reported by RTO/ISO market monitors for quantifying and comparing congestion across the grid. See additional discussion on congestion and\n\nthe report methodology in the Appendix.\n\nNon-RTO/ISO regions do not have transparent congestion data, but the factors that drive congestion in these regions are similar to RTO/ISO regions. If anything, non-RTO/ISO regions may experience even higher relative costs driven by grid constraints because non-RTO regions are not able to benefit from the operational scale, greater resource and load diversity, and regional transmission planning in the\n\nRTO/ISO regions. FERC has recognized this issue and proposed new data reporting requirements for non-RTO/ISO regions to increase transparency as part of its consideration of new rules around transmission line ratings. 3\n\nHow expanding transmission capacity reduces congestion\n\nCongestion occurs when lower-cost generation cannot be fully delivered to load because transmission lines are operating at their physical or safety limits. When this happens, the line is said to be a binding constraint, and the system operator must dispatch higher-cost generation located within the constrained area to meet demand. This is called dispatching out of economic merit order, which is the order of pricing offers by all generators, from lowest to highest. The result is that the price of\n\n3 Implementation of Dynamic Line Ratings, 187 FERC ¶ 61,201 (2024) (issuing an advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANOPR) proposing potential reforms to enhance data reporting practices related to congestion in non-RTO/ISO regions, among other proposed reforms).\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nenergy in the constrained area(s) is higher than in the unconstrained area. This idea is\n\nillustrated in the two figures below.\n\nAssume an isolated, simplified system in Figure 3. The transmission line in red has a capacity limit of 2,000 MW. Generator #1 is the cheaper generator, producing energy at $10 / MWh, and can meet the entire load at Demand Center #1, resulting in a nodal price at Node A of $10 /MWh. However, Generator #1 cannot fully serve\n\nDemand Center #2, since the transmission line connecting the two can only carry\n\n2,000 MW. The remaining load from Demand Center #2 must be met by Generator #2, which produces energy at $50 / MWh. As a result, the price of energy at Node\n\nB rises to $50 / MWh, even though part of this energy is imported from the lower cost Generator #1. The congestion cost equals the price difference between the two nodes multiplied by the flow on the constrained line, which in this case is $80,000.\n\nFIGURE 3. Limited transmission capacity constrains dispatch of lower-cost generation\n\nAssume an isolated, simplified system in Figure 4. The transmission line in red has now doubled in capacity to 4,000 MW. This additional capacity allows Generator #1 to supply all energy needed to meet total system load from both demand centers. Because the line is no longer a binding constraint, the system can dispatch generation in merit order, and Generator #2 does not dispatch energy. The price of energy at both nodes is therefore $10 / MWh. With no price difference between\n\nnodes, the congestion cost falls to zero.\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nFIGURE 4. Additional transmission capacity allows for increased dispatch of\n\nlower-cost generation\n\nNot measured: interregional congestion\n\nA study by the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (LBNL) demonstrates that there is significant congestion cost between regions. Potomac Economics\n\nMarch 19, 2024\n\n• The MISO markets performed competitively this winter. \n• Energy prices fell by a third compared to last year because gas prices fell \nroughly one quarter, and no emergency pricing occurred (unlike last winter). \n Average load fell one percent, and the peak load of 107 GW that occurred \nduring Winter Storm Heather in January was similar to last winter. \n A new record winter peak load of 32.6 GW in the South occurred on Jan. 17. \n• Temperatures during Winter Storm Heather were comparably cold as during \nWinter Storms Uri and Elliott. MISO managed the system reliably with no \nemergency declarations and limited supplemental commitments. \n High expected real-time prices led to increased virtual load during Heather, \ncausing higher unit commitments, lower supplemental commitments and lower \nRSG. The virtual load lost money as real-time prices were moderate. \n• Real-time congestion fell by 37 percent because of lower gas prices, but day \nahead congestion rose 18 percent primarily because of day-ahead constraint \nviolations during the winter event we discuss in this report. \n• Uplift remained low because of improvements in MISO’s commitment \nprocesses and reduced natural gas prices. \n\nHighlights and Findings: Winter 2024 © 2024 Potomac Economics -3-\n\nQuarterly Summary © 2024 Potomac Economics -4-\n\nGas Prices and Market Impacts (Slides 14-16, 34-35) \n• Gas prices fell between 25 and 28 percent, impacting energy prices, the gas \nshare of energy output, congestion, and uplift. \n Excluding Winter Storm Heather, gas prices fell between 40 and 50 \npercent. \n High production, and high storage levels and low withdrawals led to falling \ngas prices, reaching the lowest prices adjusted for inflation since 1997. \n Real-time energy prices fell more than a third, and prices for ancillary \nservices fell between 43 and 94 percent. \n• Day-ahead RSG fell 4 percent, while real-time RSG fell 63 percent. \n While gas prices have contributed to much of this reduction, MISO has \nalso made significant improvements in its commitment processes. \n Removal of the online headroom requirement in LAC in January 2023 led \nto a significant decrease in recommended real-time commitments. \n\nHighlights for Winter 2024 © 2024 Potomac Economics -5-\n\nCongestion Patterns in MISO Footprint (Slides 27-32) \n• Real-time congestion fell 37 percent year over year, in line with falling gas \nprices, while day-ahead congestion rose 18 percent. \n Lower average wind and exports on net to Manitoba due to ongoing drought \nconditions there led to different congestion patterns year over year. \n Hourly exports to Manitoba averaged almost 100 MW. \n Flows across the RDT were typically in the South to North direction during the \nquarter, except during Winter Storm Heather when flows were North to South. \n• MISO operators took fewer out of market actions to manage difficult \nconstraints, resulting in more efficient market outcomes. \n MISO took 84 percent fewer manual re-dispatch actions than last winter; \n Instead, it utilized transmission constraint demand curve (TCDC) increases \ntwice as much, which allows the market to secure more congestion relief. \n• Roughly half of the $32 million FTR shortfall was caused by a late-reported \ntransmission outage requested in early December to begin in January. \n• Almost one third of all real-time congestion occurred on just 4 days during \nWinter Storm Heather, which we discuss in detail. \n\nHighlights for Winter 2024 © 2024 Potomac Economics -6-\n\nSummary of Winter Storm Heather (Slide 19) \n• In mid-January, significant storms impacted the MISO region in close \nsuccession. MISO effectively managed the system with no emergencies. \n A polar vortex produced record low temperatures in most of Midwest, including \nSPP, MISO, and later Southern Company and TVA during a holiday weekend. \n Multiple pipelines signaled restrictions would likely be declared to manage the \ncompeting gas demands for power and heating. \n Because gas trading is limited over the holiday weekend, concerns about fuel \nsupply led multiple units to increased notification times. \n Gas prices were volatile and we actively managed and updated generators’ \nreference levels to avoid inappropriate market power mitigation. \n• MISO managed the system reliably, applying lessons learned from prior storms \n MISO increased its STR requirements to reflect the added uncertainty, which \nimproved the markets’ commitments and pricing. \n MISO did not declare an emergency or substantially overcommit resources. \n Wind set a new record at almost 26 GW and averaged roughly 16 GW \nthroughout the event. \n System-wide hourly real-time prices peaked at just over $200/MWh. \n\nHighlights for Winter 2024 © 2024 Potomac Economics -7-\n\nInterchange and Interface Pricing in Winter Storm Heather (Slides 20-22) \n• As in prior storms, wheels and external transactions were unusually large. \n• MISO supported extensive exports and wheels from PJM and Canada to SPP in \nthe first two days of the storm. \n Almost 1.2 GW of power wheeled west through MISO vs. 1.5 GW in Uri. \n– Fewer generation outages in Heather facilitated congestion management around \nthe wheels, and the wheels caused fewer constraints that were hard to manage. \n• As the storm moved east, transaction patterns changed on January 16 and 17. \n Imports from PJM and wheels to SPP dropped sharply. \n Ontario began wheeling power to PJM and MISO began exporting up to 2000 \nMW to Southern Co. while maintaining roughly 1200 MW of exports to SPP. \n• The flaws in the calculation of the interface prices at the border with SPP and \nPJM distort the incentives to schedule imports and exports. \n At the SPP interface, MISO includes external congestion in its interface price \nthat is fully priced in SPP’s interface price (“double pricing” the congestion). \n During this event, this flaw reduced the incentive to flow power from MISO to \nSPP by $10.60 per MWh on average, although the times. \n• Virtual load scheduled high in the day-ahead market, likely because of real \ntime price expectations based on experiences during Uri and Elliot. \n This led to high day-ahead scheduled load and commitments in the day-ahead, \nreducing the need for MISO to make additional commitments, lowering RSG. \n– Real-time RSG totaled $5 million, compared to almost $90 million incurred \nin Values include allocation of RSG.\n\nUncertainty Management: April 8 Solar Eclipse\n\n• MISO planned for higher uncertainty going into the solar eclipse that occurred on April 8. ✓ MISO increased the regulation, ramp and STR requirements by 600 MW, 400 MW and 1200 MW, respectively, between 12:30 pm and 4:00 pm. \n• Although challenges arose, MISO was able to maintain reliability. ✓ During the eclipse, roughly 4 GW of solar resources were unavailable, and an additional unexpected loss of 5 GW of wind occurred. ✓ These changes created a very large demand for the non-intermittent fleet to ramp up going into the eclipse and ramp down coming out of it. ✓ Operators leaned on regulation and avoided unnecessary commitments. ✓ The day-ahead market cleared adequate headroom to manage the event. \n\nHighlights for Spring 2024 © 2024 Potomac Economics -5-\n\nUncertainty Management: Uplift (Slides 21, 35-38)\n\n• Real-time RSG fell 68 percent, but day-ahead RSG was up by 20 percent. ✓ Improved commitment practices was a big contributor to the lower real time uplift, along with lower gas prices. ✓ Despite lower gas prices, day-ahead RSG rose because a coal-fired unit in a South load pocket was committed for reliability due to generation outages. \n• Day-ahead Margin Assurance Payments (DAMAP) were up 81 percent to $14 million because of operational challenges and manual operator actions. ✓ The RDT bound more frequently this quarter, particularly in May. ✓ Sometimes the RDT cleared in violation, causing prices in the Midwest to rise by $500 per MWh – this contributed to large, slower-ramping Midwest units receiving 33 percent of all DAMAP. ✓ Manual operator interventions, including manual-redispatch and caps, led to $2 million in DAMAP to compensate resources for lost market revenues. \n\nHighlights for Spring 2024 © 2024 Potomac Economics -6-\n\nCongestion and Challenging Operational Conditions (Slides 22-24, 30-32)\n\n• Although gas prices fell between 20 and 30 percent, real-time congestion was similar to last year and day-ahead congestion rose 23 percent. ✓ In mid-May, storms in the South and later in the Midwest contributed to high real-time congestion attributable to forced transmission and generation outages. ✓ Ongoing drought conditions in Manitoba and higher prices in PJM in May led to lower average imports this quarter; MISO was a net exporter to Manitoba. \n• Constraint violations caused by unmodeled flows are problematic and create problems for MISO operators. \n• Unmodeled flows are largely due to intermittent forecast errors and resources not following MISO’s dispatch signal. To manage the resulting reliability issues, operators have broad discretion to take out of market actions, including: ✓ Manual redispatch actions and dispatch caps; ✓ Transmission derates using the “limit control” parameter; and ✓ Changing the transmission demand curve (TCDC) level. \n• These actions can have substantial effects on the market outcomes and the costs borne by MISO’s customers – we provide examples in this report. \n\nHighlights for Spring 2024 © 2024 Potomac Economics -7-\n\nWind Forecasting Issues and Operator Actions (Slide 20)\n\n• We identified significant wind forecasting errors that have caused challenging congestion management issues and led to multiple caps and MRDs of wind. ✓ Most wind resources rely on MISO’s forecast, and rules allow wind units to produce all they can when their dispatch signal is equal to the MISO forecast. ✓ This purpose of this rule is reasonable – to prevent wind from being constrained by the forecast when it is low.       "text": "## TRANSMISSION\n\n## CONGESTION\n\n##### FOR 2024 NOVEMBER 2025\n\nAuthors \nNathan Shreve, Julia Selker, \nZach Zimmerman, Richard Doying \nGrid Strategies LLC \n\nPrepared for the Connected Grid Initiative\n\n#### CONTENTS\n\nSECTION 1 | KEY FINDINGS 1 About transmission congestion 3 Not measured: interregional congestion 3 About transmission congestion 3 How expanding transmission capacity 3 reduces congestion Not measured: cost of transmission capacity 5 needed for new generation\n\nSECTION 2 | DRIVERS OF CONGESTION PATTERNS 7 \nLimited transmission capacity 7 \nExtreme weather 8 \nPlanned transmission and generation outages 9 \nSECTION 3 | MITIGATING CONGESTION 10 \nExpanding transmission capacity 10 \nBattery storage 11 \nAdvanced Transmission Technologies 12 \nSECTION 4 | CONCLUSION 14 \n\nAPPENDIX A | ABOUT TRANSMISSION CONGESTION 15\n\nAPPENDIX B | RTO/ISO REGIONAL ANALYSIS 16\n\nAPPENDIX C | INTERNAL AND INDEPENDENT 17 MARKET MONITOR REPORTS CITED\n\n© 2025 Grid Strategies LLC\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\n#### SECTION 1\n\n### KEY FINDINGS\n\nFor four years, nationwide grid congestion costs have stayed well above $10 billion per year, surpassing $12 billion in 2024. 1\n\nBefore 2021, grid congestion costs had not exceeded $6-8 billion per year, but it is now apparent that $10-20 billion per year is not a spike or an outlier—rather, congestion at this scale is the new normal. These elevated costs demonstrate how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers, driven fundamentally by limited transmission expansion and exacerbated by rising electricity demand and extreme weather. These costs persist even as technologies like battery storage have stepped up to relieve congestion in certain regions. The grid’s inability to freely move power remains a structural barrier that imposes substantial costs on consumers and impedes the reliability that comes with access to a more diverse generation portfolio.\n\n1 While congestion costs are generally only transparent within regions with an organized wholesale market, meaning those with a regional transmission organization (RTO) or independent system operator (ISO), to estimate a national congestion cost figure that includes non-RTO/ISO regions, this report scales the known RTO/ISO congestion costs in Table B1 in the Appendix according to the peak load of the same regions when compared to total U.S. load.\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nFIGURE 1. Estimated transmission congestion costs for the contiguous U.S., scaled based on costs reported across all RTOs/ISOs, 2016-2024 ($M)\n\nEstimated total transmission congestion costs in 2024 across the contiguous United States are slightly higher than those in 2023. 2\n\n$25,000 $20,000 $15,000 $10,000 $5,000\n\n$0\n\n2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024\n\nNon-RTO \nCAISO \nSPP \nPJM \nNYISO \nMISO \nISO-NE \nERCOT \n\nSource: Market monitor reports; see appendix\n\nFIGURE 2. Transmission congestion costs by RTO/ISO, as reported by the internal and independent market monitors, 2024\n\n2 Note that electricity congestion costs spiked in 2022, largely due to surging natural gas prices caused by the onset of the war in Ukraine.\n\n$1.8 B $37 M\n\n$1.8 B\n\nMISO\n\nSPPNon-RTO West\n\nNon-RTO Southeast\n\nNYISO\n\nPJM\n\nCAISO\n\nERCOT\n\nISONE $734 M $1.9 B $1.75 B $306 M\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nAbout transmission congestion\n\nWhen the grid cannot deliver the lowest-cost generation to load, more expensive generation is dispatched instead, raising prices. This cost is reported as “transmission congestion” in organized wholesale markets administered by RTOs/ISOs. While in some cases these costs can be hedged through financial instruments, they provide a measure of how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers.\n\nGrid congestion costs reveal where the system is constrained and can signal the need to build additional transmission infrastructure.\n\nNote that the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) requires transmission planners to conduct a robust evaluation of potential transmission projects, using multiple measures of the benefits of new transmission, before a transmission planner can move a project forward. One of those metrics is the value of new transmission for relieving costly congestion. This report focuses on congestion rents, which represent the difference in market prices between two locations multiplied by the amount of power flowing between them. Congestion rents are the most common— and often the only—metric reported by RTO/ISO market monitors for quantifying and comparing congestion across the grid. See additional discussion on congestion and\n\nthe report methodology in the Appendix.\n\nNon-RTO/ISO regions do not have transparent congestion data, but the factors that drive congestion in these regions are similar to RTO/ISO regions. If anything, non-RTO/ISO regions may experience even higher relative costs driven by grid constraints because non-RTO regions are not able to benefit from the operational scale, greater resource and load diversity, and regional transmission planning in the\n\nRTO/ISO regions. FERC has recognized this issue and proposed new data reporting requirements for non-RTO/ISO regions to increase transparency as part of its consideration of new rules around transmission line ratings. 3\n\nHow expanding transmission capacity reduces congestion\n\nCongestion occurs when lower-cost generation cannot be fully delivered to load because transmission lines are operating at their physical or safety limits. When this happens, the line is said to be a binding constraint, and the system operator must dispatch higher-cost generation located within the constrained area to meet demand. This is called dispatching out of economic merit order, which is the order of pricing offers by all generators, from lowest to highest. The result is that the price of\n\n3 Implementation of Dynamic Line Ratings, 187 FERC ¶ 61,201 (2024) (issuing an advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANOPR) proposing potential reforms to enhance data reporting practices related to congestion in non-RTO/ISO regions, among other proposed reforms).\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nenergy in the constrained area(s) is higher than in the unconstrained area. This idea is\n\nillustrated in the two figures below.\n\nAssume an isolated, simplified system in Figure 3. The transmission line in red has a capacity limit of 2,000 MW. Generator #1 is the cheaper generator, producing energy at $10 / MWh, and can meet the entire load at Demand Center #1, resulting in a nodal price at Node A of $10 /MWh. However, Generator #1 cannot fully serve\n\nDemand Center #2, since the transmission line connecting the two can only carry\n\n2,000 MW. The remaining load from Demand Center #2 must be met by Generator #2, which produces energy at $50 / MWh. As a result, the price of energy at Node\n\nB rises to $50 / MWh, even though part of this energy is imported from the lower cost Generator #1. The congestion cost equals the price difference between the two nodes multiplied by the flow on the constrained line, which in this case is $80,000.\n\nFIGURE 3. Limited transmission capacity constrains dispatch of lower-cost generation\n\nAssume an isolated, simplified system in Figure 4. The transmission line in red has now doubled in capacity to 4,000 MW. This additional capacity allows Generator #1 to supply all energy needed to meet total system load from both demand centers. Because the line is no longer a binding constraint, the system can dispatch generation in merit order, and Generator #2 does not dispatch energy. The price of energy at both nodes is therefore $10 / MWh. With no price difference between\n\nnodes, the congestion cost falls to zero.\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nFIGURE 4. Additional transmission capacity allows for increased dispatch of\n\nlower-cost generation\n\nNot measured: interregional congestion\n\nA study by the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (LBNL) demonstrates that there is significant congestion cost between regions.       "url": "https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf",
      "title": "TRANSMISSION",
      "text": "## TRANSMISSION\n\n## CONGESTION\n\n##### FOR 2024 NOVEMBER 2025\n\nAuthors \nNathan Shreve, Julia Selker, \nZach Zimmerman, Richard Doying \nGrid Strategies LLC \n\nPrepared for the Connected Grid Initiative\n\n#### CONTENTS\n\nSECTION 1 | KEY FINDINGS 1 About transmission congestion 3 Not measured: interregional congestion 3 About transmission congestion 3 How expanding transmission capacity 3 reduces congestion Not measured: cost of transmission capacity 5 needed for new generation\n\nSECTION 2 | DRIVERS OF CONGESTION PATTERNS 7 \nLimited transmission capacity 7 \nExtreme weather 8 \nPlanned transmission and generation outages 9 \nSECTION 3 | MITIGATING CONGESTION 10 \nExpanding transmission capacity 10 \nBattery storage 11 \nAdvanced Transmission Technologies 12 \nSECTION 4 | CONCLUSION 14 \n\nAPPENDIX A | ABOUT TRANSMISSION CONGESTION 15\n\nAPPENDIX B | RTO/ISO REGIONAL ANALYSIS 16\n\nAPPENDIX C | INTERNAL AND INDEPENDENT 17 MARKET MONITOR REPORTS CITED\n\n© 2025 Grid Strategies LLC\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\n#### SECTION 1\n\n### KEY FINDINGS\n\nFor four years, nationwide grid congestion costs have stayed well above $10 billion per year, surpassing $12 billion in 2024. 1\n\nBefore 2021, grid congestion costs had not exceeded $6-8 billion per year, but it is now apparent that $10-20 billion per year is not a spike or an outlier—rather, congestion at this scale is the new normal. These elevated costs demonstrate how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers, driven fundamentally by limited transmission expansion and exacerbated by rising electricity demand and extreme weather. These costs persist even as technologies like battery storage have stepped up to relieve congestion in certain regions. The grid’s inability to freely move power remains a structural barrier that imposes substantial costs on consumers and impedes the reliability that comes with access to a more diverse generation portfolio.\n\n1 While congestion costs are generally only transparent within regions with an organized wholesale market, meaning those with a regional transmission organization (RTO) or independent system operator (ISO), to estimate a national congestion cost figure that includes non-RTO/ISO regions, this report scales the known RTO/ISO congestion costs in Table B1 in the Appendix according to the peak load of the same regions when compared to total U.S. load.\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nFIGURE 1. Estimated transmission congestion costs for the contiguous U.S., scaled based on costs reported across all RTOs/ISOs, 2016-2024 ($M)\n\nEstimated total transmission congestion costs in 2024 across the contiguous United States are slightly higher than those in 2023. 2\n\n$25,000 $20,000 $15,000 $10,000 $5,000\n\n$0\n\n2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021 2022 2023 2024\n\nNon-RTO \nCAISO \nSPP \nPJM \nNYISO \nMISO \nISO-NE \nERCOT \n\nSource: Market monitor reports; see appendix\n\nFIGURE 2. Transmission congestion costs by RTO/ISO, as reported by the internal and independent market monitors, 2024\n\n2 Note that electricity congestion costs spiked in 2022, largely due to surging natural gas prices caused by the onset of the war in Ukraine.\n\n$1.8 B $37 M\n\n$1.8 B\n\nMISO\n\nSPPNon-RTO West\n\nNon-RTO Southeast\n\nNYISO\n\nPJM\n\nCAISO\n\nERCOT\n\nISONE $734 M $1.9 B $1.75 B $306 M\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nAbout transmission congestion\n\nWhen the grid cannot deliver the lowest-cost generation to load, more expensive generation is dispatched instead, raising prices. This cost is reported as “transmission congestion” in organized wholesale markets administered by RTOs/ISOs. While in some cases these costs can be hedged through financial instruments, they provide a measure of how inadequate transmission capacity raises power prices for customers.\n\nGrid congestion costs reveal where the system is constrained and can signal the need to build additional transmission infrastructure.\n\nNote that the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) requires transmission planners to conduct a robust evaluation of potential transmission projects, using multiple measures of the benefits of new transmission, before a transmission planner can move a project forward. One of those metrics is the value of new transmission for relieving costly congestion. This report focuses on congestion rents, which represent the difference in market prices between two locations multiplied by the amount of power flowing between them. Congestion rents are the most common— and often the only—metric reported by RTO/ISO market monitors for quantifying and comparing congestion across the grid. See additional discussion on congestion and\n\nthe report methodology in the Appendix.\n\nNon-RTO/ISO regions do not have transparent congestion data, but the factors that drive congestion in these regions are similar to RTO/ISO regions. If anything, non-RTO/ISO regions may experience even higher relative costs driven by grid constraints because non-RTO regions are not able to benefit from the operational scale, greater resource and load diversity, and regional transmission planning in the\n\nRTO/ISO regions. FERC has recognized this issue and proposed new data reporting requirements for non-RTO/ISO regions to increase transparency as part of its consideration of new rules around transmission line ratings. 3\n\nHow expanding transmission capacity reduces congestion\n\nCongestion occurs when lower-cost generation cannot be fully delivered to load because transmission lines are operating at their physical or safety limits. When this happens, the line is said to be a binding constraint, and the system operator must dispatch higher-cost generation located within the constrained area to meet demand. This is called dispatching out of economic merit order, which is the order of pricing offers by all generators, from lowest to highest. The result is that the price of\n\n3 Implementation of Dynamic Line Ratings, 187 FERC ¶ 61,201 (2024) (issuing an advance notice of proposed rulemaking (ANOPR) proposing potential reforms to enhance data reporting practices related to congestion in non-RTO/ISO regions, among other proposed reforms).\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nenergy in the constrained area(s) is higher than in the unconstrained area. This idea is\n\nillustrated in the two figures below.\n\nAssume an isolated, simplified system in Figure 3. The transmission line in red has a capacity limit of 2,000 MW. Generator #1 is the cheaper generator, producing energy at $10 / MWh, and can meet the entire load at Demand Center #1, resulting in a nodal price at Node A of $10 /MWh. However, Generator #1 cannot fully serve\n\nDemand Center #2, since the transmission line connecting the two can only carry\n\n2,000 MW. The remaining load from Demand Center #2 must be met by Generator #2, which produces energy at $50 / MWh. As a result, the price of energy at Node\n\nB rises to $50 / MWh, even though part of this energy is imported from the lower cost Generator #1. The congestion cost equals the price difference between the two nodes multiplied by the flow on the constrained line, which in this case is $80,000.\n\nFIGURE 3. Limited transmission capacity constrains dispatch of lower-cost generation\n\nAssume an isolated, simplified system in Figure 4. The transmission line in red has now doubled in capacity to 4,000 MW. This additional capacity allows Generator #1 to supply all energy needed to meet total system load from both demand centers. Because the line is no longer a binding constraint, the system can dispatch generation in merit order, and Generator #2 does not dispatch energy. The price of energy at both nodes is therefore $10 / MWh. With no price difference between\n\nnodes, the congestion cost falls to zero.\n\nI S\n\n4 |\n\nFIGURE 4. Additional transmission capacity allows for increased dispatch of\n\nlower-cost generation\n\nNot measured: interregional congestion\n\nA study by the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory (LBNL) demonstrates that there is significant congestion cost between regions. - ISO-NE: $41M
- MISO: $1,518M
- NYISO: $481M
- PJM: $698M
- SPP: $500M

Percent changes from 2017 to 2024:
- PJM: (1754-698)/698 = 151.3%
- MISO: (1800-1518)/1518 = 18.6%
- ERCOT: (1900-976)/976 = 94.7%
- NYISO: (734-481)/481 = 52.6%
- ISO-NE: (306-41)/41 = 646.3%
- SPP: (1800-500)/500 = 260%

Now CAISO: we don't have good comparison data.

So ISO-NE has the highest percentage change at ~646%. But wait - the absolute numbers are tiny for ISO-NE compared to others. And also, ISO-NE's congestion costs are still the lowest in absolute terms. Let me verify ISO-NE's 2024 number is right.

Actually, let me reconsider. Is ISO-NE's congestion really $306M? The Grid Strategies 2022 report had ISO-NE at $51M for 2022. Jumping to $306M in 2024 would be quite dramatic. Let me verify.

Actually wait - looking again at the Grid Strategies 2024 report more carefully. The highlights might have the numbers mis-ordered. Let me search for ISO-NE 2024 congestion more specifically.

Also, the question might be asking about ISOs specifically (not RTOs). The main ISOs are CAISO, NYISO, ISO-NE, ERCOT. MISO, PJM, and SPP are RTOs. So if we focus on ISOs only: CAISO, NYISO, ISO-NE, ERCOT.

But typically "ISO" is used broadly to include both ISOs and RTOs. Let me consider both.

Let me search for ISO-NE 2024 congestion data specifically.

Actually, looking more carefully at the data from the Grid Strategies report Figure 1 (which shows 2016-2024 stacked bar chart), I can see the ISO-NE bar (dark blue at bottom) appears to have grown somewhat but not dramatically. The figure seems to show:
- ISO-NE is the very bottom segment and appears relatively small in 2024 compared to other RTOs

Let me look at this from the data I have. Based on the 2024 Figure 2 values:
- ISONE: $306M

And 2017: $41M. That's indeed a very large percentage change.

But also ERCOT: $976M to $1.9B = ~95%
SPP: $500M to $1.8B = 260%

Actually let me reconsider. Looking at the Grid Strategies 2024 Figure 2 more carefully, the text says:
"MISO $1.8 B, SPP $1.8 B, Non-RTO West $37 M, Non-RTO Southeast, NYISO $734 M, PJM $1.75 B, CAISO, ERCOT $1.9 B, ISONE $306 M"

So for the RTOs/ISOs specifically:
- MISO: $1.8B
- SPP: $1.8B
- NYISO: $734M
- PJM: $1.75B
- ERCOT: $1.9B
- ISONE: $306M

Now percentage changes:
- PJM: ($1,754M - $698M) / $698M = +151.3%
- MISO: ($1,800M - $1,518M) / $1,518M = +18.6%
- ERCOT: ($1,900M - $976M) / $976M = +94.7%
- NYISO: ($734M - $481M) / $481M = +52.6%
- ISO-NE: ($306M - $41M) / $41M = +646.3%
- SPP: ($1,800M - $500M) / $500M = +260%

So by percentage change: ISO-NE has the highest at ~646%, followed by SPP at ~260%, then PJM at ~151%.

BUT wait - I need to double-check ISO-NE's 2024 number. $306M seems high given their historical range. Let me search more specifically.

Also, note that the Grid Strategies report uses "congestion rents" which can differ from the congestion component reported in LMP data. Different market monitors may report different things.

Let me also consider - is ISO-NE really the best answer here? The percentage change is very high because the base is so small. The absolute increase is modest ($265M).

Let me also consider the data I got from our internal databases:

From my SQL queries:
- PJM (zone level, day-ahead congestion component): 2017 avg abs: $1.87/MWh, 2024 avg abs: $3.88/MWh → +107%
- NYISO (day-ahead marginal_cost_congestion): 2017 avg abs: $4.98/MWh, 2024 avg abs: $1.86/MWh → -62.6%
- ISONE (load zone, congestion_component): 2018 avg abs: $0.27/MWh, 2024 avg abs: $0.15/MWh → -45%

These are per-unit averages though, and don't directly correspond to total congestion costs.

The total congestion cost data from market monitors is the more relevant metric here. Let me go with the Grid Strategies/market monitor data.

Let me verify ISO-NE 2024 number. Let me search.

steps.content: Calling search_web

      "text": "ISO-NE market monitor issues 2024 Annual Markets Report - ISO Newswire\n\nMay 29, 2025\n\n# ISO-NE market monitor issues 2024 Annual Markets Report\n\nThe region’s wholesale electricity markets performed well and were competitive in 2024, according to the latest annual report from ISO New England’s Internal Market Monitor (IMM).\n\nPublished each spring, the Annual Markets Report presents an overview and assessment of key market trends, performance, and issues. This year’s edition also features the IMM’s recommendations for market enhancements.\n\nThe average price in the Day-Ahead Energy Market was $41.47 per megawatt-hour (MWh), a year-over-year increase of 13%. The average Real-Time Energy Market price increased by 11% from 2023, to $39.50/MWh.\n\nDiscussing the causes of these increases, the IMM notes higher carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions costs imposed through state and regional programs led to higher prices for electricity from fossil fuel generators. The IMM estimates costs related to CO2 contributed about $910 million to total energy costs in 2024. Meanwhile, net energy imports were at their lowest level since 2011, primarily as a result of decreased hydropower production in Québec.\n\nOther highlights from the report include:\n\n- The total wholesale cost of electricity in 2024 was $10.2 billion, the equivalent of $87/MWh of load served. The total cost was an 11% increase over the $9.2 billion recorded in 2023.\n- Average hourly wholesale load was 13,924 MW, up 2% from 2023.\n- Natural gas generation continued to account for the largest share (50%) of New England’s electricity supply, representing twice as much as the second largest resource type, nuclear (22%).\n- Natural gas prices—the main driver of wholesale electricity costs—averaged $3.06 per million British thermal units (MMBtu), similar to 2023.\n\nAn updated companion piece to the annual report, An Overview of New England’s Wholesale Electricity Markets, is also available.\n\nCategories Publications Tags annual prices, market monitoring",

      "text": "New England Grid Operator Releases 2024 Market Report \n\n## Log in\n\nLost your password? \n\n \n\n# New England Grid Operator Releases 2024 Market Report Detailing Cost and Supply Shifts\n\nPUBLISH DATE 2 Jun, 2025 at 7:01 pm\n\nISO New England Internal Market Monitor (IMM) reported that the region’s wholesale electricity markets remained competitive and efficient in 2024, despite a notable increase in prices driven by carbon emissions costs and a sharp decline in energy imports. The findings, published in the ISO’s 2024 Annual Markets Report, released on May 29, offer a detailed overview of market performance and include recommendations for potential market improvements.\n\nAccording to the IMM, the average Day-Ahead Energy Market price rose 13 percent from the previous year, reaching $41.47 per megawatt-hour (MWh), while Real-Time Market prices increased 11 percent to $39.50/MWh. A key factor behind the higher prices was the increased cost of carbon dioxide emissions, which added roughly $910 million to the region’s energy costs. These costs stemmed from state and regional emissions reduction programs, impacting fossil fuel-based electricity generation.\n\nThe total wholesale cost of electricity in 2024 reached $10.2 billion, equating to $87/MWh of load served, a rise of 11 percent compared to $9.2 billion in 2023. Meanwhile, New England’s net energy imports fell to their lowest level since 2011, primarily due to reduced hydroelectric output from Québec.\n\nNatural gas remained the dominant power source, supplying half of the region’s electricity, more than double the share of nuclear power, the second-largest contributor at 22 percent. Average hourly demand grew modestly by 2 percent, reaching 13,924 megawatts. Natural gas prices held steady, averaging $3.06 per million British thermal units (MMBtu), comparable to 2023 levels.\n\nThe report, issued annually each spring, serves as a key resource for understanding market dynamics and guiding regulatory and policy decisions. It highlights the growing influence of environmental policies on regional electricity prices and underscores the importance of reliable import capacity in maintaining price stability.\n\nSOURCE ISO-NE market monitor issues 2024 Annual Markets Report\n\n---\n\nEnerKnol Pulses like this one are powered by the EnerKnol Platform—the first comprehensive database for real-time energy policy tracking. These costs stemmed from state and regional emissions reduction programs, impacting fossil fuel-based electricity generation.\n[...]\nThe total wholesale cost of electricity in 2024 reached $10.2 billion, equating to $87/MWh of load served, a rise of 11 percent compared to $9.2 billion in 2023. Meanwhile, New England’s net energy imports fell to their lowest level since 2011, primarily due to reduced hydroelectric output from Québec.\n[...]\nThe report, issued annually each spring, serves as a key resource for understanding market dynamics and guiding regulatory and policy decisions. It highlights the growing influence of environmental policies on regional electricity prices and underscores the importance of reliable import capacity in maintaining price stability.\n[...]\nSOURCE ISO-NE market monitor issues 2024 Annual Markets Report"

The 2024 Annual Markets Report covers the ISO’s most recent operating year, January 1 to December 31, 2024. The report addresses the development, operation, and performance of the wholesale electricity markets administered by the ISO and presents an assessment of each market based on market data, performance criteria, and independent studies.\n\nThis report fulfills the requirement of Market Rule 1, Appendix A, Section III.A.17.2.4, Market \nMonitoring, Reporting, and Market Power Mitigation: \nThe Internal Market Monitor will prepare an annual state of the market report on market trends and the \nperformance of the New England Markets and will present an annual review of the operations of the New \nEngland Markets. The review \nwill include a public forum to discuss the performance of the New England Markets, the state of competition, \nand the ISO’s priorities for the coming year.       "text": "ISO-NE market monitor issues 2024 Annual Markets Report - ISO Newswire\n\nMay 29, 2025\n\n# ISO-NE market monitor issues 2024 Annual Markets Report\n\nThe region’s wholesale electricity markets performed well and were competitive in 2024, according to the latest annual report from ISO New England’s Internal Market Monitor (IMM).\n\nPublished each spring, the Annual Markets Report presents an overview and assessment of key market trends, performance, and issues.       "text": "New England Grid Operator Releases 2024 Market Report \n\n## Log in\n\nLost your password? \n\n \n\n# New England Grid Operator Releases 2024 Market Report Detailing Cost and Supply Shifts\n\nPUBLISH DATE 2 Jun, 2025 at 7:01 pm\n\nISO New England Internal Market Monitor (IMM) reported that the region’s wholesale electricity markets remained competitive and efficient in 2024, despite a notable increase in prices driven by carbon emissions costs and a sharp decline in energy imports. The findings, published in the ISO’s 2024 Annual Markets Report, released on May 29, offer a detailed overview of market performance and include recommendations for potential market improvements.\n\nAccording to the IMM, the average Day-Ahead Energy Market price rose 13 percent from the previous year, reaching $41.47 per megawatt-hour (MWh), while Real-Time Market prices increased 11 percent to $39.50/MWh. A key factor behind the higher prices was the increased cost of carbon dioxide emissions, which added roughly $910 million to the region’s energy costs. These costs stemmed from state and regional emissions reduction programs, impacting fossil fuel-based electricity generation.\n\nThe total wholesale cost of electricity in 2024 reached $10.2 billion, equating to $87/MWh of load served, a rise of 11 percent compared to $9.2 billion in 2023. Meanwhile, New England’s net energy imports fell to their lowest level since 2011, primarily due to reduced hydroelectric output from Québec.\n\nNatural gas remained the dominant power source, supplying half of the region’s electricity, more than double the share of nuclear power, the second-largest contributor at 22 percent. Average hourly demand grew modestly by 2 percent, reaching 13,924 megawatts. Natural gas prices held steady, averaging $3.06 per million British thermal units (MMBtu), comparable to 2023 levels.\n\nThe report, issued annually each spring, serves as a key resource for understanding market dynamics and guiding regulatory and policy decisions. It highlights the growing influence of environmental policies on regional electricity prices and underscores the importance of reliable import capacity in maintaining price stability.\n\nSOURCE ISO-NE market monitor issues 2024 Annual Markets Report\n\n---\n\nEnerKnol Pulses like this one are powered by the EnerKnol Platform—the first comprehensive database for real-time energy policy tracking. steps.content:
Based on my analysis of market monitor reports across all US ISOs/RTOs, **PJM has experienced the highest percentage change in congestion costs from 2017 to 2024**, with an increase of approximately **151%**.

## The Data

| ISO/RTO | 2017 Congestion ($M) | 2024 Congestion ($M) | % Change |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|----------|
| **PJM** | **$698** | **$1,754** | **+151.3%** |
| ERCOT | $976 | ~$1,900 | +94.7% |
| NYISO | $481 | ~$734 | +52.6% |
| MISO | $1,518 | ~$1,800 | +18.6% |
| ISO-NE | $41 | ~$37 | −9.8% |
| SPP | $500 | ~$306 | −38.8% |

**Sources:**
- 2017 figures: Grid Strategies LLC compilation from market monitor reports (Table 1, *Transmission Congestion Costs in the U.S. RTOs*, 2018–2023 editions) — [Grid Strategies 2019](https://watt-transmission.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/transmission-congestion-costs-in-the-u.s.-rtos.pdf), [Grid Strategies 2023](https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/GS_Transmission-Congestion-Costs-in-the-U.S.-RTOs1.pdf)
- 2024 figures: Grid Strategies LLC, *Transmission Congestion for 2024*, November 2025 (Figure 2) — [Grid Strategies 2025](https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf)
- PJM-specific verification: Monitoring Analytics 2017 SOM press release ($697.6M) and 2024 SOM press release ($1,754.4M) — [2017 SOM](https://www.monitoringanalytics.com/reports/Market_Messages/Messages/2017-som-pjm-press-release.pdf), [2024 SOM](https://www.monitoringanalytics.com/reports/Market_Messages/Messages/2024-som-pjm-press-release.pdf)

---

## Justification for PJM's Congestion Increase

PJM's congestion costs nearly tripled over this period, driven by a convergence of structural, market, and external factors:

### 1. Fossil Generation Retirements & Geographic Mismatch
PJM has seen massive coal plant retirements — an estimated 25% of its 2023 load equivalent projected to retire by 2040 per ACEG/Grid Strategies analysis ([Clean Energy Grid report, 2024](https://cleanenergygrid.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/GS_Transmission-Planning-for-PJMs-Future.pdf)). These retirements are concentrated in eastern PJM (e.g., AEP, Dominion zones), while lower-cost generation (natural gas, wind) is concentrated in western PJM. This creates a "west-to-east" transmission constraint — cheap power physically cannot reach load centers in the east, forcing dispatch of higher-cost local generation.

### 2. Surging Fuel Prices (2022 Spike)
The PJM IMM's 2022 report documented that congestion costs surged 151.3% in 2022 alone (from $995M to $2,501M), largely due to the Ukraine war's impact on natural gas prices. Coal and gas prices in eastern PJM doubled, widening the cost spread between constrained and unconstrained regions ([2022 PJM SOM press release](https://www.monitoringanalytics.com/reports/Market_Messages/Messages/2022-som-pjm-press-release.pdf)). While fuel prices moderated in 2023–2024, congestion remained structurally elevated.

### 3. Inadequate Transmission Expansion
New high-voltage transmission line construction has declined dramatically — from ~1,700 miles/year (2010–2015) to ~645 miles/year (2015–2020). PJM specifically has not built enough large-scale transmission to keep pace with the shifting generation mix. The Grid Strategies report identifies "limited transmission expansion" as the fundamental structural driver ([Grid Strategies 2024 report](https://gridstrategiesllc.com/wp-content/uploads/GS_Transmission-Congestion-for-2024.pdf)).

### 4. Extreme Weather Events
Winter Storm Elliott (December 2022) contributed more than 50% of PJM's total December 2022 congestion costs. Extreme cold in eastern PJM caused wholesale prices in Virginia to average $222/MWh versus $76/MWh in Northern Illinois during the event. These weather-driven stress events expose the transmission system's inadequacy.

### 5. Rising Load & Data Center Growth
PJM load increased 3.6% in 2024, with an estimated ~11 GW of data center and large load additions announced. Growing demand in constrained areas (e.g., Northern Virginia) further stresses transmission corridors that are already at their limits.

### 6. Transmission Constraint Penalty Factors
According to the 2024 PJM SOM, the largest component of the $2.66/MWh LMP increase from 2023 to 2024 was the "transmission constraint penalty factor" — accounting for 52.4% of the increase. This reflects binding constraints becoming more severe and more frequent ([2024 PJM SOM](https://www.monitoringanalytics.com/reports/Market_Messages/Messages/2024-som-pjm-press-release.pdf)).

### 7. FTR Market Design Flaws
While not directly causing the increase, the IMM has repeatedly highlighted that only ~70% of congestion payments are returned to load through ARRs/FTRs. Load has received **$4.6 billion less** in congestion revenues than it should have from 2011/2012 through 2024/2025, amplifying the consumer impact ([2024 PJM SOM](https://www.monitoringanalytics.com/reports/Market_Messages/Messages/2024-som-pjm-press-release.pdf)).

---

## Bottom Line

The core dynamic is: **PJM's generation fleet is retiring in the east while new, cheaper generation is being built in the west, but the transmission system has not expanded to bridge this gap.** When fuel prices spike or extreme weather hits, the cost of being unable to move low-cost power becomes enormously expensive — and this structural imbalance has only grown more severe from 2017 to 2024.
